content.thinking:
Turn is Th. Board: Js 3h 7c Th. I have As Kh - I now have a gutshot to the broadway straight (Q makes broadway), and the backdoor flush didn't come. Actually I need a Q for the straight. That's 4 outs. Plus I have overcards - an A or K beats a pair of Jacks/Tens... wait, villain may have hit two pair or a set.

Villain is betting 60 into 140 (43% pot) with only 108 stack. They're pot-committed essentially. If I raise, they'll likely call off. I have AK - 6 outs to top pair plus 4 outs to the straight = ~10 outs = about 20% equity on river.
 
The pot odds are asking for 30% to call, but I'm only getting about 20% equity with my 10 outs. Villain's got 48 chips left after betting 60, so if I call, I'm probably calling down the river too. The math is tight here - AK is right on the borderline, though villain's loose-passive tendencies make them a candidate for bluffing or thin value.

But looking at their line, they've been aggressive on both streets, which is unusual for a passive player. That suggests they actually have something. I'm folding.

Villain's PFR is 22%, so this 3.5x raise is within their range but somewhat strong. With A2s, I have decent equity. To call costs 68 more into a pot of 72. The pot odds are 68/(72+68) = 49%, and A2s vs a 22% range has roughly 45-50% equity.

Villain is loose-passive, wins a lot at showdown (38/48 = 79%). The large raise suggests strength. With A2s I have a flush draw possibility and an ace, but the pot odds are borderline.


I'm calling here since A2s has enough playability and villain's loose tendencies mean they could easily be overplaying a wider range than their raise suggests.
